S04.content:
Here’s a targeted 5-minute “cheat sheet” on Lakehouse architecture—enough to sound savvy in your interview without drowning in details.

1. What Is a Lakehouse?  
- Hybrid of data lake (cheap, flexible storage of raw data) + data warehouse (schema, ACID, BI/SQL performance)  
- Key idea: run BI, SQL, ML, streaming on one unified platform  

2. Core Components  
- Storage layer: object store (S3, ADLS, GCS) or HDFS for raw files (Parquet/ORC/Avro)  
- Transactional metadata/engine: Delta Lake, Apache Iceberg or Hudi transaction log for ACID, time travel, schema evolution  
- Catalog/Metastore: Hive Metastore, AWS Glue, Unity Catalog – keeps track of tables, partitions, schemas  
- Compute/query engines: Spark, Presto/Trino, Snowflake, Databricks SQL, Azure Synapse—separate compute from storage  

3. Key Features to Mention  
- ACID transactions & versioning on object storage  
- Schema enforcement + on-write or on-read validation  
- Support for batch & streaming (unified pipeline)  
- Time travel & data lineage  
- Separation of storage (elastic, cheap) vs compute (scaled to workload)  
- Open formats → avoids vendor lock-in  

4. Typical Logical Layers (Medallion Pattern)  
- Bronze: raw ingested data (logs, IoT, APIs)  
- Silver: cleaned, conformed (joins, dedupe, basic aggregates)  
- Gold: curated, high-value (aggregates/feature tables for BI & ML)  

5. Performance & Optimization  
- Data skipping/z-ordering or partition pruning to avoid full scans  
- File compaction (small files → optimal read performance)  
- Caching (e.g., in-memory or SSD) for hot tables  
- Indexing or bloom filters (Iceberg/Hudi)  

6. Governance & Security  
- Fine-grained access control via catalogs (Unity Catalog, Lake Formation)  
- Encryption at rest/in transit  
- Auditing & lineage tracking (who accessed/changed what)  
- Data masking, column-level security  

7. Why Organizations Love It  
- Cost-efficient: low-cost storage + elastic compute  
- Flexibility: ingest any format, evolve schemas  
- Unified analytics: BI + ML on same data store  
- Open ecosystem: plug-in best-of-breed engines  

8. Challenges / Gotchas  
- Metadata scaling—very large tables → watch out for catalog bottlenecks  
- Managing small files in streaming—need auto-compaction  
- Query performance tuning (partition design, file sizes)  
- Maturity varies: Delta vs Iceberg vs Hudi feature gaps  

9. Sample “Sound-Bite” Answer to “Why Lakehouse?”  
“I’d describe a Lakehouse as the next-gen data platform uniting the best of lakes and warehouses. You get low-cost, flexible object storage with open formats, yet still benefit from ACID transactions, schema enforcement, time-travel and high-performance SQL—all in one place.”  

10. Prep Questions They Might Ask  
- How do Delta Lake (or Iceberg/Hudi) maintain ACID on object stores?  
- Explain schema evolution vs schema enforcement.  
- How would you design the bronze/silver/gold layers for a streaming pipeline?  
- Compare data skipping (z-order) vs partition pruning.  
- What’s your approach to securing sensitive data in a lakehouse?  

Memorize the 3–4 key bullets in each section. Use real-world examples (e.g., “We built a 3-layer pipeline in Databricks using Delta Lake on S3 with Unity Catalog…”). Good luck!

- A distributed system can at best simultaneously provide two of the three guarantees: consistency, availability, and partition tolerance.  
- Consistency ensures that all nodes return the same, most recent data after each write operation.  
- Availability guarantees that every request receives a response, without guaranteeing it contains the latest data, even in the face of node failures.

S06.content:
Below is a high-level comparison of the two dominant “real-time” concurrency schemes, plus a rough recommendation for a small team that’s starting from scratch.

1. Core idea  
   • Operational Transformation (OT)  
     – You send user-made operations (insert “A” at pos 5, delete range 10–12, etc.) to a central (or peer-to-peer) server.  
     – When concurrent ops arrive, you “transform” one op against the other so they commute—i.e. so each client ends up in the same final state.  
   • Conflict-free Replicated Data Types (CRDTs)  
     – Each client holds a local replica of the document plus metadata (timestamps, unique IDs, tombstones, trees, etc.).  
     – Edits mutate the local replica immediately; the resulting local operation or state-diff is broadcast.  
     – Merging two replicas is by design associative+commutative+idempotent, so all replicas converge without an explicit transform step.  

2. Maturity & ecosystem  
   • OT  
     – Google Docs, Dropbox, ShareDB, Etherpad, Mobwrite.  
     – Decades of research; battle-tested on large documents.  
     – But core OT engines are non-trivial to implement correctly (edge cases around undo/redo, formatting, multi-range ops).  
   • CRDT  
     – Gaining traction since ~2016; popular JS libraries: Yjs, Automerge, Slate-CRDT, Swarm.  
     – Simpler mental model (you’re just merging state) but the data structures (tombstones, identifier-trees) carry overhead.  
     – Excellent offline/“local-first” support by design.  

3. Implementation complexity  
   • OT  
     – Need a core transform function that handles every pair of concurrent ops.  
     – You must maintain per-client history buffers, vector clocks or sequence IDs, and possibly a central server.  
     – Undo/redo, rich text, and formatting layers add complexity.  
   • CRDT  
     – Pick a sequence CRDT variant (RGA, Logoot, LSEQ, Treedoc, WOOT, etc.) or use an existing library.  
     – Must manage metadata bloat (tombstones or growing identifier trees) and possibly garbage-collect it.  
     – Merging is simpler, but if you roll your own, getting a performant identifier scheme is tricky.  

4. Performance & resource trade-offs  
   • OT  
     – Very low per-op metadata; ops are small.  
     – Centralization (or peer synchronization) can reduce client-side memory.  
     – But transform logic can become a CPU/latency bottleneck under high concurrency.  
   • CRDT  
     – Metadata overhead grows roughly O(n) or O(n log n) in document length, depending on tombstone strategy.  
     – Local ops are instantaneous, even offline; merges may be heavier but can be batched.  
     – Scales nicely in pure peer-to-peer models.  

5. Team skill & time to market  
   • OT  
     – If you have strong background in concurrency control and everyone’s OK with a central server, OT can be lean and mature.  
     – However, building a rock-solid OT engine (including formatting, undo/redo, cursor sync, etc.) is a multi-month effort.  
   • CRDT  
     – Conceptually easier to reason about because you don’t write explicit “transform” code—state just merges.  
     – Rolling your own is still subtle; best path is to adopt a well-maintained library (Yjs, Automerge) and build your editor on top.  

Recommendation for a small team starting from scratch  
  – Unless you really need a zero-metadata or massively centralized model, lean toward a CRDT-based solution.  
     * You get instant local edits, offline support, automatic convergence.  
     * Mature, open-source frameworks (Yjs or Automerge) let you focus on the UI/editor API, not the core CRDT logic.  
  – If you already have a trusted central server and you want the absolute smallest per-op footprint or you’re extending an existing OT engine, then go OT—but be prepared for a steep implementation curve.  

Bottom line: for a greenfield, small-team effort, pick a CRDT library (e.g. Yjs) and integrate it into your editor. You’ll ship faster, avoid subtle transform bugs, and get offline-first collaboration “for free.”
